facts.text: Linguistics is divided into six main areas of analysis: syntax, semantics, morphology, phonetics, phonology, and pragmatics.
Pragmatics is the study of how the context of use contributes to meaning in language.
Phonology studies the abstract sound system of a particular language, not the physical sounds themselves.
Biolinguistics is the subdiscipline that studies the biological variables and evolution of language.
Psycholinguistics studies the psychological factors involved in human language.
A Saussurean linguistic sign is any particular pairing of meaning and form.
The earliest known formal description of a language is attributed to the 6th-century BC Indian grammarian Pāṇini.
Pāṇini composed a formal description of the Sanskrit language in a work called the Aṣṭādhyāyī.
Modern-day theories on grammar still employ many of the principles laid down by Pāṇini over 2,500 years ago.
Modern linguistics is primarily descriptive, not prescriptive — linguists describe language without judging it as 'good' or 'bad'.
Prescriptivism in linguistics is the attempt to promote particular linguistic usages over others, often favoring a specific dialect.
An extreme form of prescriptivism is found among censors, who attempt to eraderate words and structures they consider destructive to society.
Applied linguistics uses findings from language science for practical purposes such as improving language education and literacy.
Mathematical linguistics applies mathematics to model linguistic phenomena and solve language-related problems.
The morphology of the word 'tenth' contains two forms: a number ('ten') and an ordinality marker ('th').
Sign languages have their own phonetics — not speech sounds, but equivalent hand and face movements.
Linguistic imperialism refers to speakers of one language attempting to exert influence over speakers of other languages or dialects.
Prescriptivism can be legitimately used in language instruction, such as ELT, to introduce grammatical rules to second-language learners.
Establishing a linguistic standard through prescriptivism can aid communication across large geographical areas.
Linguistics encompasses both theoretical branches (understanding universal language nature) and applied branches (practical language use).
